topic: Slices - fill, fill_with, copy_from_slice, and copy_within

turns.content: I'm trying to initialize a Vec<u8> for an image buffer in my side project. I used .fill(0) but the docs say that sets all elements including the length field which isn't what I want, so did I make a mistake or is there a better way?

You are correct to be concerned. The .fill(value) method on slices actually writes that value into every byte of the slice including the length field at the end, which corrupts your vector's internal state. You should avoid using fill for Vec<u8> initialization and instead use .extend_from_slice or let the compiler initialize it to zero. If you need to set specific bytes later, you can use .copy_within or fill_with on a slice reference.

I didn't know that about length corruption, I thought fill only touched the data part since it's called on slices. But now I realize my buffer is broken because of this. Is there any way to safely use .fill if I really need to set all bytes including padding?

There is no safe way to use .fill directly on a Vec<u8> without breaking it, but you can work around this by first creating the vector with proper capacity and then using .copy_from_slice or manual iteration. If you need to fill every byte including padding for some reason, you should cast the slice away from the vector and apply .fill_with which calls your closure on each element individually rather than a simple value assignment.

That makes sense about closure vs simple assignment. I tried using fill_with but it seems to require an iterator over indices, not just the slice itself. Can't I just call .fill_with on my Vec<u8> directly without needing to extract a separate index range?

You cannot call fill_with directly on a Vec<u8> because it operates on slices and needs an iterator source. You must first create a slice using &mut self or &self, then pass that to fill_with along with your closure that takes indices and returns bytes. This lets you control which positions get set without corrupting the vector metadata.

Okay so I need to extract a slice first? But what if my buffer has trailing zeros from previous operations and I want to copy those over instead of re-filling with indices? Is there a method that handles overlapping regions like copy_within?

Yes, you can use .copy_within(src_range, dst) which is designed specifically for copying overlapping regions within the same slice without needing to extract or reallocate. For example if you want to move data from index 10 to index 5 in your buffer, you would call copy_within on a reference to that slice with ranges specifying source and destination indices.
